sentences.text: Yes.
We were all together, in a group.
And he started-- I remember he was speaking in German.
And I didn't know German very much, but I knew it.
My-- my sister knows, [INAUDIBLE].
And he told us to-- that we are free, that war finish.
He's going away.
I don't exactly remember the words, what he said.
But that was-- he was telling-- I know that if he got a-- oh, yes, we signed some paper, what the Jews made, that list for him, that he was saved, he was good to us, or something like that.
I know that we signed it.
He got this, and he disappeared.
And he went, and we'd been alone.
And then the Russian come on the horses.
And we sit on the ground somewhere, together.
And they said, war is finished.
War is-- you are free.
You are free.
And well, we didn't know what to do.
And my-- my husband gets better.
That was, again, day, or two, or three.
He get better.
Then he got his accordion again, and he went to this Turkish country somewhere, and he play.
And while he's play, he got some bread.
And-- and they, straight away, set up some camp.
I don't know.
Who was it?
Red Cross, I think.
Then they boiled some soup or something for us.
You went to this camp?
When we'd been in the camp, that-- some were there.
I don't know now.
They bring, to us, some big container with soup.
And then we have to do whatever everyone wanted to do.
We didn't know what to do.
It was some wagons.
There was some train.
And we went on the train-- the cattle train.
And it was open.
No, it wasn't closed, just half.
And then the train stops somewhere.
Brunnlitz was not very far from Cracow.
But for us, that town seems very, very long journey.
We want to go back to Cracow.
That was only-- only place when I was hoping to see my sister and, and start a life again.
That only one, only place I knew.
And my sister didn't know-- Manci, Manci once, when we stopped, she couldn't wait.
She said, go to the country.
We went, and she stole a horse, a horse with a buggy.
And we travel on this for sometimes.
I have to drink some water.
Excuse me.
Oh, never mind.
Then-- And then we go again, on a train.
Sorry, I need to drink some water.
Thank you.
[DRINKS] OK, and then somehow, we went to Cracow.
We [? found ?] Cracow.
[INAUDIBLE].
[DRINKS]
I tell you what, I never tell anybody that much, as I'm telling you.
I'm surprise myself, because when Kenneally came here, I didn't want to talk to him at all.
But you make me talk.
OK, what else?
Yeah.
Oh, I mean, few days, that takes.
It should take, maybe, a few hours-- only two days or three days.
And first, we went to [POLISH].
That was the suburb of Cracow, when the ghetto was-- but it wasn't any Jewish there.
But Regina [INAUDIBLE] her mate up there.
She [INAUDIBLE]-- Regina was sister of my husband, and Manci was sister-in-law.
And she let us in, and we went in the kitchen, a big kitchen somewhere, and gives it something to eat or to drink.
And Regina was desperate at Manci, because there were not-- oh, didn't I tell you about the Brunnlitz?
They took away [?
Herman ?] and Olek, because they have two boys.
And they didn't stay in Brunnlitz.
Oh, I forgot to tell you this.
They didn't stay in Brunnlitz.
They have to go back, and they went to Auschwitz or-- I think Auschwitz.
That was Regina's husband, and Poldek's brother, Henry.
Henry wasn't in Brunnlitz-- he was just for a day or two.
And unfortunately, Schindler couldn't do anything to stop them, because they have two little boys with them.
Richard, ?] Regina's son was maybe, I don't know, two years old or something, three years old, three years old, very young.
Olek was the older, and they were not in Brunnlitz with us.
That's when Poldek was playing by himself for Schindler, not with Henry.
And when we-- when the war finish and we went there, that Regina was desperate.
They didn't know if her husband is alive or not, and so was Manci.
They got to-- [INAUDIBLE] [?
Richard ?] was a son, and daughter Niusia was with her.
But Manci has only one son, Olek, [?
Olek. ?] And they didn't know if they are alive.
They started to look everywhere.
Already, we find out that in Cracow was some office for Jewish people to find about the other.
I don't know how they call it.
And they went there, and they tried to find him.
And we stay with that woman for a few days.
And then they all said, what about if you go to the place, [POLISH], where you live before?
Then we went there, and I went to this-- I find that-- that woman who was taking care of the house before the war, where we live.
[POLISH] was her name.
And she knew.
She said, yes, it is one apartment.
You can go there.
And we went to the apartment, but it wasn't actually ours.
It was different, but the same building.
And we took another couple with us, because of what-- again, we were afraid that two people, it was two bedroom.
We took another couple.
And [INAUDIBLE] started to play.
It was a cabaret on Florianska Street in Poland.
And he didn't have his suit, I believe.
Oh, and again, I didn't tell you about the-- the pocket watch Schindler gave us.
For each of us, it was ready, in brown paper-- a big packet with material, and pajama, and some little things.
Each one of the prison-- each one of us get this.
That was on the end of the war, in Brunnlitz.
And with that, we have with us.
And you know that this material was made for a suit for my husband.
And it's still in Melbourne, the suit, then he got it, my grandson.
And I got a suit made for me.
Manci made it, because she was dressmaker.
And well, he start to play, and he start to earn money.
And he bought me some clothes.
He bought me pants and [INAUDIBLE].
And we started to live in Cracow.
Well, that was a long story.
He was-- she was waiting for a long time.
I don't know what happened [INAUDIBLE].
She was sewing.
She was a dressmaker with some other friend.
And she lives on the second floor from the cabaret where Poldek was working, Florianska.
And I go there very often, and she didn't know where is Henry.
You know, after the war, was no post.
It wasn't on the telephone.
Nothing was working normally.
We couldn't send a letter or anything like that.
It was such a chaos.
But Regina found-- Regina was first, who find out about Richard.
She found out that Richard is with some family.
She was coming from Auschwitz, from orphanage, and she saw somewhere-- I don't know, pictures?
It wasn't in pictures.
Anyhow, she knew it.
She went to the family.
The family name was [?
Liebling. ?] And they-- they have Richard.
And Olek was there, and she found Richard and Olek.
But that was before Manci.
Manci still didn't know about Henry.
And then, eventually, somehow it comes, news, that he's in Germany, because he was-- he was liberated by American, not by Russian.
He was in different camp.
And he was in Munich, Munchen, Munich, yes.
And he was already there.
And of course, straight away, she went to Germany.
How, I don't know.
How she get there-- because it was a border.
How did she goes there, I don't know.
But she went to Germany, and she met him.
And we stayed, and we living.
And we stayed, but when my husband was working in Florianska, I didn't know [INAUDIBLE] about my sister.
And one evening-- he used to play for long at night-- I was with Manci.
And he came up with my sister.
He opened the door, and here she was.
[CRYING] [DRINKS] OK.
[CRYING]
I try to be brave, OK?
[CRYING] OK, now, that's how I met my sister.
And she lived with us, and she was looking, and her husband was not-- she was married before me, in ghetto, very short time.
She marry [INAUDIBLE].
But he went away, maybe, after two or three months, being her husband.
And then she was going, as the other Jews, to that [POLISH], we call it-- the Jewish place where people were looking for family.
It was a place like this, in [POLISH].
And then when she [? met ?] her husband, Josef Gross-- he was a Gross.
He was looking for his wife and his child, but he never find them.
And that's how they met, and they come live with us in [POLISH].
And Manci-- oh, that was after the war, already.
What happened to--
My brother?
My brother left Brunnlitz, and he went somewhere, because-- because he was scared that he was Jewish policeman [INAUDIBLE].
And he went to Poland, but different part of Poland.
[POLISH], it was-- different-- different city.
And he was a dentist by profession, and he started to work up there.
And we didn't have much contact, because the post didn't work right after the war.
Anyhow, he stayed there.
And that's a-- there another, completely nother story.
He married a woman, and she was killed after the war.
He remarry, and he had children.
That is whole different story, of my brother's life.
But I was with my sister.
And Poldek want to go to Munchen, to Munich.
He wants to be with his brother.
And we decided to go to Germany.
My sister stay with Josef.
They married in civil war, but-- civil wedding [INAUDIBLE], register wedding, yeah?
And we were trying to go to German-- to Munchen, but we couldn't go because we didn't have any paper.
And on the way, they stop us.
They stop us in a place, Kudowa, that was holiday resort in Poland.
And it got the-- UNRRA started.
You know what UNRRA was?
No, it was short for United Nation Refuge, something [? camp. ?] It was Jewish people, and they start to work already, that UNRRA.
And we got support from UNRRA, all of us, right away.
And when we'd been in Kudowa, we are told that not to say anything.
We are not Polish.
We are not Jewish.
We are Greek or something like this.
I don't know what it was.
Anyhow, we stay in Kudowa, and Leo started to work again, in Kudowa.
He was playing someplace and having money.
And I stay and-- of course, at night-- in Europe you play not a long time, till morning.
I don't know.
I don't remember what I do.
It-- I know there were young men.
I met a man, [POLISH], [POLISH].
And his uncle, [?
Adam-- ?] that was another sister-in-law, [INAUDIBLE] was his younger brother's wife, which marry in Munchen, that his younger brother married in Munchen, Munich.
And well, we were-- we were waiting.
We were waiting, every day, waiting to go further, to go to-- eventually, we were getting some paper, stamp, and we went to-- landed in Munchen, in Munich.
End of '45, maybe, something like this, yes.
I think that's about-- the end of '45.
[INAUDIBLE].
We'd been in Munchen for, again, maybe 8 months or 10 months.
And now we went to live with Henry and Manci.
And of course, Olek was there.
And it was in [GERMAN], I remember.
And they were working-- Poldek and Henry were working in [GERMAN].
Yes, I remember the name.
It was a place when they play on the stage, both of them.
I stay in Munchen, and-- you know, Schindler was there.
Schindler was living with them when [? we ?] came.
And he would have-- he got a Jewish girlfriend, and he was still very handsome.
I remember he was staying with us, and he was drinking a lot.
He loves a good cognac.
And-- and we all smoke cigarette that time.
We have [INAUDIBLE].
We have chocolate, and we have cigarette.
And well, we didn't do much.
We didn't do much.
I-- I find out that I'm pregnant.
That was in Munchen.
I was-- I was always, you know, the one who was-- I don't know how-- I don't know what I was.
I'm still shy or whatever.
And it was-- I was scared to take the food, because it didn't belong to me, you know?
We live with Henry, with Manci, and [INAUDIBLE].
And was-- I remember that bowl of apple.
And I want it so much, but I didn't want to take it without asking.
I was craving for food.
I was pregnant.
And then my sister decided-- and she came to me, to Munich, my sister with her husband.
And again, we stayed in the same apartment.
But my brother-in-law was a jeweler, and he start-- he was looking around.
I don't know.
He didn't start to work.
He didn't want to stay in Germany.
He couldn't take it.
He was very upset when he heard the German talking, when he go on the street.
He got terrible upset.
It was his wife, and child, and all family, so many brother and sister.
And he want to go out.

And Henry wants to go out too, and they-- they apply for the visa to America, because he's-- one of the brother was already in America, George Rosner, [NON-ENGLISH] Rosner, from before the war [INAUDIBLE].
And I don't know if Manci got a permit and [? handed ?] it, to go to America.
We were waiting for the permit.
We didn't get it.
I don't know why.
It was probably because she was German-- she was born in Vienna.
Anyhow, they went to America, and we stay in Germany.
And as my brother-in-law didn't want to stay there, we applied to visa for Venezuela, I think.
But we find out that Leo got some cousin in Paris.
And they went first, to Paris, and then we joined, and then we been in Paris.
And that's when Anna was born, in [FRENCH] hospital.
Yes, it was a such experience for me.
I didn't know what it mean to be pregnant.
I didn't know what to be mother.
I didn't know language.
And it-- food wasn't-- wasn't opened.
You couldn't go to shop to buy milk or anything.
We have special cart for mother who were feeding babies.
And I went to this with the cart.
I put the cart, and I took them.
Later on, they said to my sister, poor, poor young woman-- she's deaf and dumb-- because I didn't know French.
And I-- I have to find out in Paris.
We have very little.
First, we had a big apartment, what his cousin let him have it.
But I want to be with my sister, and they didn't know that his cousin, who live in Paris, [INAUDIBLE] my sister.
And we have-- my brother, [INAUDIBLE], bought-- pay him, not bought-- he paid him money to get to some apartment at a flat, right?
It was one room and a kitchen, nothing more, very tiny, very small.
My sister had the baby five weeks before me, a baby boy.
She was in private hospital, and she was a very hard labor.
And, well, I went to the Lafayette to buy a big teddy bear.
I went to the hospital.
My tummy is already big, and they just thought that I'm going to have a baby.
That was fun.
And then five week after, I went to the [FRENCH] hospital.
And three and a half year, we live in Paris.
I learn a little bit French after.
My sister knew French from before the war-- not much, but still, she was studying.
She was the one who was finishing [INAUDIBLE].
She got degree.
I didn't.
And she managed in Paris with her French, but I didn't speak French at all when-- I have to find out and learn everything.
First of all, [? it was ?] hard to bring up baby.
That was-- I was [? mad. ?] I was really-- I-- what I did, I-- I didn't know that you have to burp the baby, with the wind.
And I was leaning over her with my breast, not to move the baby.
[LAUGHS] And I always thought that she's hungry, and she's hungry, and she's hungry.
I want her to eat, and to eat.
And then when we start to cook already, and I wanted to eat.
And she said, she's not eating.
She is vomiting.
And she was beautiful.
She was red-cheeked, and she was so beautiful.
But I always somehow thought she has not enough to eat.
And Leo got a engagement to Chamonix, for playing there.
Beautiful, we have an apartment and a guest house, of course, on the very top for the stuff.
I would never be able to, because he was working.
And I was cooking porridge.
I was cooking semolina or whatever and feeding the baby, all the time.
And then, yes--
From Paris, we come to Australia.
My sister went first, again.
My sister went because, again, my brother-in-law didn't want to be in Europe at all.
And it was some far relation, some cousin of my mother or whatever, and-- and they sent a permit.
And my sister came to Australia.
We stay in Paris.
Is My husband was earning very good money in Paris, and he was playing in many places-- [INAUDIBLE], or [INAUDIBLE], other places.
He was earning good money, and he buying me things.
And then in Chamonix was casino event, but I never went down.
Not a few months, and I want to go to Paris too.
And my husband wants to go to America, because his brother were in America.
My sister was in Paris.
But maybe he loves me so much, I don't know, that we went to Paris.
We went to Paris after a few months.
My sisters sent me a letter with a photo.
And the photo was taken in [INAUDIBLE], somewhere, where the palms are.
And when I seen that, that was like a dream for me.
It was a beach, and sand, and sea.
First time I see sea, in my life, when we went to [INAUDIBLE] Australia.
We went by train.
From-- from Paris to Australia, on was ship.
But first, we went by train to [?
Genoa. ?] And then we took a ship, and takes us six weeks, I believe.
Sirenia ?] was the name of the ship.
And when-- it was a nice ship.
I was never seasick.
Some other people were, but-- and my husband was playing on the ship again, and makes everybody happy.
And it's a few people here who come with us, two or three, on the same ship.
When did we--
To Williams-- Williamsport, [INAUDIBLE].
In Melbourne, yes, Melbourne, yes-- Henry was waiting for us-- my sister, and my brother-in-law, and some other family.
Of course, they didn't have car.
I don't know how.
And-- but she got, already, house, my sister.
When she sent us the letter with the photos, in beginning, they live in boarding house somewhere.
About six months they'd been here, before we arrive.
But my brother-in-law started to work.
And he got, somehow, enough money to put deposit on the house.
And the letter was, my house is your house.
And we lived together for-- in the same house [INAUDIBLE] for six years, one kitchen.
We started to buy furniture, a little bit.
I started to work.
Oh, it was-- well, it was hard.
I was working in a-- without English, again, I start with the people who are only English, in a small-- small, few machine.
Want year?
What year?
I think was '49? '49, I think we arrived to Melbourne.
And then I went to [INAUDIBLE] manufactory, a good, big factory here in Richmond, and I was strong.
I was working all day.
I came home by train.
I went back to the city for English, to that adult education.
And I have time from [INAUDIBLE]-- well, I was young and stronger, and I have time for everything.
I was cooking, and baking, and scrubbing, and washing.
But my sister didn't work, and that was good, because she looks after Anna.
And she take her to kindergarten, she comb her hair, and she was-- my husband wasn't working during the day.
It was my husband and my sister together.
Sometimes was fun, when they went to shop.
They said, funny that the girls look completely like father and the boys like mother.
[LAUGHS] Because [?
Louie ?] was always with us.
He was like-- my sister didn't have enough milk.
I have to feed [?
Louie. ?]
I have Frances, younger one, born in Australia, in Melbourne-- was six years after.
But we still live together, and that was a comfort.
I have Frances in St.
Margaret [INAUDIBLE] hospital.
It was private, and I had silk pajama.
And my husband come with the flowers, because in [FRENCH] hospital, I remember, when Anna was born, he-- it was visiting hours, and nobody comes to me.
You know why?
Because it was such a big hospital, he was walking around the hospital with the flower.
He didn't know I have baby already.
Anyhow, but [? he did in ?] Melbourne.
I was in luxury at hospital, and I had beautiful pajamas, and I got flowers.
And there were not allowed children.
Anna was standing on the [INAUDIBLE].
Margaret hospital, when I had Frances, it was 42 years ago, or 43, she will be.
The children were not allowed to visit the mother, and I was from the window.
And after, when Frances was still in the pram-- maybe she was six months or eight months-- we started to look for the separate house.
And we found a duplex, because I want to be with my sister, always together.
So so many years we'd been together, they put together money-- my husband and my brother-in-law.
And we had one kitchen, and we never was any problem.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Bless you.
And no problem at all-- I was always with my sister, and the duplex was [INAUDIBLE].
And we've been, again, together, like door to door.
And Frances was born, and Frances was growing.
And that's [? my ?] [? life. ?] And then Anna got three boys, and Frances got three girls.
And I'm a happy grandmother.
I just read, not long ago, they said old age is not the wintertime.
It's a harvest time.
And I think that's wonderful, because I got my grandchildren.
Now we are not having the dinner, but we used to have, every Monday, dinner for 15 people.
My husband was the cook.
I was the help.
And we set up the table, and we-- they have come together, and we have a lovely time, yeah.
Yeah.
I tell you what, when I see the news, what is happening, I am very bitter.
I thought that that war will be-- will be that lesson for the whole earth.
I was thinking that finished.
People don't-- wouldn't kill each other anymore.
Why?
And I-- I'm-- I'm terribly nervous, you see.
Since I came to Melbourne, since even before, I start to take the relaxing tablets.
I went-- come to Melbourne, I went to Dr.
[INAUDIBLE]-- Valium, or Librium, or Valium, or Librium, because I have bad dreams, and that bad dreams.
And even now, from time to time-- not long ago, I had a dream that German are pulling me by hair.
And it's so many years.
But in the beginning, I didn't talk about this.
I didn't want to talk.
And honestly, I thought that-- gone, it's finished.
That-- that it's finished, nobody will know about it, what we went through.
And it's all-- but somehow, it-- I don't know why.
It comes out.
Some people want to talk [INAUDIBLE] that when Kenneally came to us.
And he was sitting here with Leo, talking, and I went to garden.
I said I didn't want to say anything.
I make them a cup of tea, and again I went to-- but eventually, even before-- yes, after the Kenneally that was-- that was made by [INAUDIBLE] from television, that was documentary.
And he cames here, and I didn't, again, want to talk to him.
He come to the kitchen, but he was so nice.
He come with me to the kitchen, and he talk-- ask me a few question.
It was documentary, [? made ?] [? him. ?] Well, [INAUDIBLE] start to talk, and I start to talk.
And my children was, why didn't you tell us?
Why didn't you tell us?
Especially my grandchildren, [INAUDIBLE], [? and ?] some things I-- I want to just say finish, no more.
But it [? comes back. ?] And I'm talking too much now, am I?
And so many things come to my mind.
I don't know what a message is.
No, I really don't know.
I-- I wish the world-- it won't be any more war.
It won't be any more killing, that people will be good, because love is the main thing.
I attend yoga classes for 30 years.
And we are discussing, and we're talking, and live now, in that moment, and don't think about past, or present, or future.
Just live now.
I know all these things, but it didn't-- I cannot lie to myself, you know.
Relaxation, meditation, no, I'm reading a lot.
And I [INAUDIBLE] the tapes and with all this thing.
I know so much.
I can advise people what to do, but if it comes to me, I-- I'm hopeless.
I'm still nervous, and I'm still upset.
I'm sad very much and get scared.
I don't know why.
It stays with me, [INAUDIBLE].
Thank you.
I really didn't talk to anybody so much as I talked to you.
[LAUGHS]
Thank ?] [? you. ?]
